elements.sent-2411-001: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has proven effective in solving complex decision-making tasks across various domains, but challenges remain in continuous-time settings, particularly when state dynamics are governed by stochastic differential equations (SDEs) with jump components.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this paper, we address this challenge by introducing the Mean-Square Bipower Variation Error (MSBVE) algorithm, which enhances robustness and convergence in scenarios involving significant stochastic noise and jumps.
elements.sent-2411-003: We first revisit the Mean-Square TD Error (MSTDE) algorithm, commonly used in continuous-time RL, and highlight its limitations in handling jumps in state dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-004: The proposed MSBVE algorithm minimizes the mean-square quadratic variation error, offering improved performance over MSTDE in environments characterized by SDEs with jumps.
elements.sent-2411-005: Simulations and formal proofs demonstrate that the MSBVE algorithm reliably estimates the value function in complex settings, surpassing MSTDE's performance when faced with jump processes.
elements.sent-2411-006: These findings underscore the importance of alternative error metrics to improve the resilience and effectiveness of RL algorithms in continuous-time frameworks.
elements.sent-2411-007: Reinforcement Learning (RL) algorithms have garnered substantial attention for their efficacy in addressing intricate decision-making challenges across diverse domains, spanning robotics, finance, and healthcare.
elements.sent-2411-008: Within RL frameworks, agents iteratively learn to make sequential decisions through interactions with an environment, aiming to maximize a cumulative reward signal.
elements.sent-2411-009: At the heart of RL lies the fundamental challenge of accurately estimating the value function, which provides insights into the expected cumulative reward under a given policy.
elements.sent-2411-010: In this paper, we delve into the task of estimating the value function within RL frameworks operating in continuous-time settings, where state dynamics are delineated by stochastic differential equations (SDEs) potentially incorporating jump components.
elements.sent-2411-011: Prior works have explored various algorithms to tackle this challenge.
elements.sent-2411-012: For instance, \cite{Doya:2000 introduced the temporal-difference (TD) algorithm tailored for the continuous-time deterministic case, while \cite{jiaandzhou2022 elucidated the convergence properties of the Mean Square TD Error (MSTDE) algorithm within continuous-time stochastic settings.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: These algorithms endeavor to estimate the value function by iteratively updating a parameter vector using distinct error metrics, thereby shedding light on their convergence behavior under diverse noise conditions.
elements.sent-2411-014: The optimal parameter derived from these algorithms ensures that the estimated value function closely approximates the true value function.
elements.sent-2411-015: Our investigation commences with a thorough review of the MSTDE algorithm, which endeavors to minimize the mean-square TD error to estimate the value function.
elements.sent-2411-016: Nonetheless, we underscore its limitations when confronted with scenarios characterized by state dynamics featuring jump components.
elements.sent-2411-017: To mitigate this challenge, we introduce the Mean Square Bipower Variation Error (MSBVE) algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-018: By focusing on minimizing the mean-square quadratic variation error, the MSBVE algorithm exhibits enhanced robustness in coping with stochastic noise and jump processes within state dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-019: Through extensive simulation experiments and formal proof, we validate the efficacy of the MSBVE algorithm in accurately estimating the value function under demanding conditions marked by significant variability with jumps.
elements.sent-2411-020: Our findings unequivocally demonstrate that the MSBVE algorithm surpasses the performance of the MSTDE algorithm, especially in scenarios characterized by substantial stochastic noise with jumps, where the MSTDE algorithm struggles to converge.
elements.sent-2411-021: These results underscore the paramount importance of considering alternative error metrics, such as the mean-square quadratic variation error, to bolster the performance and resilience of RL algorithms operating in continuous-time settings.
elements.sent-2411-022: The key distinction of our work lies in its focus on robustness: while recent studies have primarily aimed to find optimal controls in jump-diffusion models, our approach seeks to develop an algorithm that ensures the estimated value function remains unaffected by jump noise, offering a more resilient solution for general SDEs with jumps.
elements.sent-2411-023: In contrast, \cite{gao2024reinforcementlearningjumpdiffusionsfinancial and \cite{Bo2024 employ randomized control methods alongside q-learning to derive the optimal policy distribution for general SDEs with jumps, differing mainly in their choice of entropy terms.}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Lastly, \cite{Denkert2024 replaces control with a random point process, applying an actor-critic algorithm to solve a randomized problem that ultimately leads to the optimal value functions for the original system.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Unlike these works, which concentrate on finding the optimal control under jump diffusions, our research addresses the challenge of value function estimation under jump dynamics, emphasizing robustness against jump noise.
elements.sent-2411-026: The remainder of this paper is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-027: Section 2 provides an overview of the setup for the RL problem within continuous-time settings.
elements.sent-2411-028: In Section 3, we delve into the detailed descriptions of the MSTDE and MSBVE algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-029: The simulation results are presented in Section 4, where we analyze and discuss the performance of these algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-030: We draw conclusions in Section 5, summarizing the key findings and implications of our study.
elements.sent-2411-031: Finally, in Section 6, we provide detailed proofs to support the theoretical underpinnings of our proposed algorithms and their convergence properties.

elements.sent-2411-110: These findings suggest that the MSBVE algorithm is more robust in the presence of stochastic process with jumps compared to MSTDE, meaning it converges to a quantity related only to the continuous part of the process even when jump components are added.
elements.sent-2411-111: The robustness enables efficient convergence to the desired parameter values even in challenging scenarios with significant variability.
elements.sent-2411-112: In this paper, we have explored the problem of estimating the value function in continuous-time reinforcement learning under stochastic dynamics with potential jump components.
elements.sent-2411-113: To address this problem by updating a parameter vector to minimize different error metrics, we revisited the Mean-Square TD Error (MSTDE) algorithm and introduced the Mean-Square Bipower Variation Error (MSBVE) algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-114: Our algorithm converges to an optimal parameter which when plugging into the estimated value function, provides a good approximation of the true value function.
elements.sent-2411-115: We have demonstrated that the MSBVE algorithm offers improved robustness and convergence behavior compared to the MSTDE algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-116: It's important to note that if the dynamics follow a SDE with no jumps, both algorithms are equivalent.
elements.sent-2411-117: In scenarios where the dynamics follow a SDE with jumps, MSBVE converges to a quantity related only to the continuous part while MSTDE converges to the quantity related to both the continuous part and the jump part.
elements.sent-2411-118: Our findings suggest that considering alternative error metrics, such as the mean-square quadratic variation error, can enhance the performance and reliability of RL algorithms in continuous-time settings, particularly when having a diffusion with jumps.
elements.sent-2411-119: Moving forward, future research could explore extensions and refinements of the MSBVE algorithm, as well as investigate its application to real-world RL problems with complex dynamics and noise conditions.
elements.sent-2411-120: Additionally, further theoretical analysis and empirical studies could provide deeper insights into the convergence properties and computational efficiency of the proposed algorithm, paving the way for advancements in continuous-time reinforcement learning methodologies.

elements.sent-2411-164: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has demonstrated considerable success in tackling complex decision-making problems across diverse domains.
elements.sent-2411-165: However, continuous-time RL remains challenging, particularly when the underlying state dynamics are modeled by stochastic differential equations (SDEs) with jump components.
elements.sent-2411-166: In this paper, we address these challenges by proposing the Mean-Square Bipower Variation Error (MSBVE) algorithm, which enhances robustness and convergence in environments with significant stochastic noise and jumps.
elements.sent-2411-167: We begin by revisiting the widely used Mean-Square Temporal Difference Error (MSTDE) algorithm and highlight its susceptibility to bias when jump components are present.
elements.sent-2411-168: In contrast, the proposed MSBVE algorithm minimizes the bipower variation error that has the form of a product of non-overlapping absolute differences, allowing it to more accurately estimate value functions in the presence of jumps.
elements.sent-2411-169: Theoretical analysis and numerical studies confirm that MSBVE outperforms MSTDE in jump-diffusion settings.
elements.sent-2411-170: These results emphasize the critical role of alternative error metrics in advancing the resilience and accuracy of RL algorithms in continuous-time stochastic systems with jumps.
elements.sent-2411-171: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has emerged as a powerful framework for solving intricate decision-making challenges across a variety of domains, including robotics, finance, and healthcare.
elements.sent-2411-172: In RL, an agent interacts with a dynamic environment and learns to select actions that maximize cumulative rewards over time.
elements.sent-2411-173: A central objective in this paradigm is the accurate estimation of the value function, which quantifies the expected return associated with each state under a given policy.
elements.sent-2411-174: Precise value function estimation is crucial for policy improvement and long-term performance.
elements.sent-2411-175: Several algorithms for estimating the value function within the RL framework have been developed for purely continuous diffusion processes.
elements.sent-2411-176: However, in practice, observed data often exhibit jumps, which makes the pure continuous diffusion component unobservable.
elements.sent-2411-177: These discontinuities pose significant challenges for standard RL algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-178: In many applications, especially in finance, the primary interest lies in learning models based on the latent continuous component.
elements.sent-2411-179: For instance, volatility processes are crucial for quantifying investment risk.
elements.sent-2411-180: Since jumps typically reflect unpredictable news events, they are often treated as noise, and attention is focused on estimating the volatility of the continuous part.
elements.sent-2411-181: At the very least, given the fundamentally different characteristics of the continuous and jump components, they should be learned separately.
elements.sent-2411-183: The latent continuous process captures the typical dynamics that we aim to learn.
elements.sent-2411-184: Therefore, it is crucial to develop robust RL estimation methods that can accurately recover the latent continuous component in the presence of jumps.
elements.sent-2411-185: We begin by reviewing the Mean Square Temporal Difference Error (MSTDE) algorithm, which aims to estimate the value function by minimizing the mean-square TD error.
elements.sent-2411-186: However, we highlight its limitations in environments with jump-driven dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-187: To overcome these challenges, we propose the Mean Square Bipower Variation Error (MSBVE) algorithm, which minimizes the bipower variation error defined as a product of non-overlapping absolute differences.
elements.sent-2411-188: This approach enhances robustness to stochastic noise and discontinuities, which leads to more accurate value function estimation in the presence of jumps.
elements.sent-2411-189: Through rigorous theoretical analysis and comprehensive simulation studies, we demonstrate the effectiveness of the MSBVE algorithm in accurately estimating the value function under challenging conditions characterized by substantial variability arising from jump components.
elements.sent-2411-190: Our results clearly show that MSBVE outperforms the MSTDE algorithm, particularly in environments where substantial jump-induced noise hinders MSTDE's convergence.
elements.sent-2411-191: These findings highlight the critical importance of adopting alternative error metrics, such as the bipower variation error, to enhance the robustness and accuracy of RL algorithms in continuous-time settings with jump components.
elements.sent-2411-192: The key distinction of our work lies in its emphasis on robustness.
elements.sent-2411-193: While recent studies have primarily focused on deriving optimal control policies in jump-diffusion settings, our approach aims to develop an algorithm that ensures the estimated value function remains resilient to jump-induced noise.
elements.sent-2411-194: This leads to a more stable and reliable estimation framework for general SDEs with jumps and helps learn the latent continuous diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-195: Unlike these works, which concentrate on finding the optimal control under jump diffusions, our research addresses the challenge of value function estimation under jump dynamics and emphasizes robustness against jump noise.
elements.sent-2411-196: Section \ref{sec-2 provides an overview of the setup for the RL problem within continuous-time settings.}
elements.sent-2411-197: In Section \ref{sec-3 , we present the detailed descriptions of the MSTDE and MSBVE algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-198: Section \ref{simulation reports the simulation results and analyzes the performance of these algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-199: In Section \ref{application , we apply the MSBVE algorithm to the mean-variance portfolio selection problem and compare its performance with MSTDE using the real intraday trading data.}
elements.sent-2411-200: Finally, we draw conclusions in Section \ref{sec-6 , summarizing the key findings and implications of our study.}

elements.sent-2411-301: In this section, we apply the proposed MSBVE algorithm and compare it with the existing MSTDE algorithm in the context of a mean-variance portfolio selection problem.
elements.sent-2411-302: We consider a financial market with two assets over the time interval $[0,T]$: the risk-free asset follows \begin{equation}dB_{t}=r_{f}B_{t}dt,\end{equation} where $r_{f}$ is the risk-free rate, and the risky asset follows \begin{equation}dS_{t}=S_{t}(\mu dt+\sigma dW_{t}),\end{equation} where $\mu$ is the drift, $\sigma$ is the volatility, and $W_{t}$ is a standard Brownian motion.
elements.sent-2411-303: Let $X_{t ^{a}$ denote the wealth of an investor who allocates weight $a_{t}$ to the risky asset at time $t$.}
elements.sent-2411-304: Following a simplified form of the classical Merton model for continuous-time portfolio selection, the wealth process then follows an SDE: \begin{equation}dX_{t}^{a}= a_{t}\left(\sigma\rho dt+\sigma dW_{t}\right),\end{equation} where $\rho=\frac{\mu-r_{f {\sigma}$ is the Sharpe ratio.}}
elements.sent-2411-305: See \citet{merton1971optimum for the derivation of portfolio dynamics in a diffusion market.}
elements.sent-2411-306: The investor aims to minimize the variance of the wealth at time $T$ while maintaining the expected value of the wealth at time $T$ at a certain level $z$.. Specifically, \begin{equation}\min_{a}Var(X^{a}_{T})\quad s.t.\ E(X_{T}^{a})=z.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-307: The theoretical optimal policy from \cite{jia2022q is given by \begin{equation}a_{t}(\rho)=-\frac{\rho}{\sigma}(X_{t}-w(\rho)),\end{equation} where $w(\rho)=\frac{ze^{\rho^{2 T -X_{0 {e^{\rho^{2 T -1}$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-308: Under this optimal policy, the wealth process becomes \begin{equation}dX_{t}= -\rho(X_{t}-w)\left(\rho dt+dW_{t}\right),\end{equation} and the corresponding value function is defined as: \begin{equation}J(t,x) =E[X_{T}^{2}|X_{t}=x]-z^{2} =(x-w)^2 e^{\rho^{2}(t-T)}-(w-z)^{2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-309: We parameterize the value function as \begin{equation}J^{\theta}(t,x)=(x-w)^2 e^{\theta^{2}(t-T)}-(w-z)^{2},\end{equation} and define the corresponding parametric policy as follows: \begin{equation}a_{t}(\theta)=-\frac{\theta}{\hat{\sigma}}(x-w(\theta)),\end{equation} where the estimated variance $\hat{\sigma ^{2}$ is given by \begin{equation}\hat{\sigma}^{2}=\frac{\pi}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n-1}|\Delta X_{t_{i}}|\cdot|\Delta X_{t_{i-1}}|.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-310: Our theoretical analysis yields two key predictions: (1) in jump-free environments, MSBVE and MSTDE should exhibit comparable performance; (2) in settings with jumps, MSBVE is expected to outperform MSTDE due to its robustness to jumps.
elements.sent-2411-311: To empirically test both settings, we perform two types of experiments: one on raw S\&P 500 data (which includes jumps), and another on data where large jumps are removed.
elements.sent-2411-312: For jump detection, we apply the threshold $\tau=4\hat{\sigma (\Delta t)^{0.47}$, and consider observations with $|\Delta X_{t |>\tau$ to be large jumps \citep{ait2020high .}}}
elements.sent-2411-313: In practice, however, this threshold is not applied directly to the wealth process $X_{t}$, but rather to the observed S\&P 500 price process $S_{t}$, since in real markets we only observe the stock price $S_{t}$ and the risk free rate $r_{f}$.
elements.sent-2411-314: Using this method, we identify days with large jumps in the dataset of 5-minute S\&P 500 data from 2010 to 2020.
elements.sent-2411-315: Notably, the period from 2012-10-01 to 2014-09-30 exhibits a higher frequency of jumps, with 195 jump days accounting for approximately 40\% of the sample.
elements.sent-2411-316: Thus, to check the effect of jumps, we focus on this two-year dataset for model training and testing.
elements.sent-2411-317: To simulate the jump-free scenario, we apply the thresholding procedure to remove large jumps.
elements.sent-2411-318: Specifically, we define the thresholded difference process as follows: \begin{equation}\Delta S_{t_{i}}^{T}= \Delta S_{t_{i}}, if |\Delta S_{t_{i}}|<\tau, 0, if |\Delta S_{t_{i}}|\geq\tau. \end{equation} We can obtain the thresholded process by summarizing the thresholded difference process $S_{t_{i ^{T =\sum_{j=0 ^{i-1}\Delta S_{t_{j ^{T}$.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-338: In this paper, we explored the problem of estimating the value function in continuous-time reinforcement learning under stochastic dynamics with potential jump components.
elements.sent-2411-339: The continuous and jump parts exhibit fundamentally different characteristics, and in many applications, our primary interest lies in learning the dynamics of the continuous component.
elements.sent-2411-340: However, the latent continuous process is not directly observable due to the presence of jumps.
elements.sent-2411-341: To address this challenge, we revisited the MSTDE algorithm and proposed a novel MSBVE algorithm, which updates a parameter vector by minimizing an alternative error metric.
elements.sent-2411-342: We have demonstrated that the MSBVE algorithm exhibits improved robustness and convergence behavior compared to the MSTDE algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-343: Importantly, when the underlying dynamics follow an SDE without jumps, the two algorithms yield equivalent results.
elements.sent-2411-344: However, when the dynamics are governed by an SDE with jump components, MSBVE places greater emphasis on the continuous part of the dynamics and is less affected by jumps, whereas MSTDE directly reflects both the continuous and jump components, often resulting in larger bias.
elements.sent-2411-345: These findings highlight that adopting alternative error metrics, such as the mean bipower variation error, can enhance the performance and reliability of reinforcement learning algorithms in continuous-time settings, particularly in the presence of jump-diffusion processes.
elements.sent-2411-346: Moving forward, future research may explore extensions and refinements of the MSBVE algorithm, as well as investigate its applicability to real-world reinforcement learning problems characterized by complex dynamics and noisy environments.
elements.sent-2411-347: Additionally, further theoretical analysis and empirical studies could yield deeper insights into the convergence properties and computational efficiency of the proposed method, thereby contributing to the advancement of continuous-time reinforcement learning methodologies.
